title: Reading and Use of English – Part 8
description: You are going to read an article about four people who volunteered for a neighbourhood energy project. For questions 47–56, choose from the people A–D. The people may be chosen more than once.

texts.A:
### Inez, The Home Survey Volunteer
Inez first joined the energy project to help neighbours reduce winter bills in older buildings without expensive renovations. She visited homes with a trained adviser, carrying a checklist, a torch and a roll of removable tape. [47]In one flat, she discovered that the worst draught came not from the windows, as the tenant assumed, but through an oversized letterbox.[/47] A simple brush strip made the room feel different the next week. [48]Her background as a civil engineer helped her explain heat loss in practical terms without blaming residents for the condition of their homes.[/48] Inez had to learn tact, because people felt embarrassed about damp corners and unpaid bills. She never photographed anything without clear written permission. Over time, she noticed that small improvements gave people confidence to ask landlords for larger repairs from owners. She says the project worked best when advice sounded like support rather than formal inspection.
texts.B:
### Mark, The Bills Workshop Helper
Mark first became involved after spotting a handwritten notice about free energy-bill workshops for worried residents on the counter of his local library. [49]The library notice was the reason he went to the first volunteer meeting.[/49] He had worked in customer service, so he was comfortable answering questions, but the subject was unexpectedly complicated. [50]He was surprised by how many residents believed switching lights off at the socket would solve bills that were actually driven by heating and poor insulation.[/50] Rather than correct people sharply, he asked them to bring old bills and compare patterns month by month. Mark found that numbers made more sense when people could circle them with a pen at the table. He also learned to avoid jargon like “standing charge” until he had shown it on the page. His favourite sessions ended with people leaving not cheerful, exactly, but less confused and less ashamed afterwards.
texts.C:
### Leila, The Data Mapper
Leila originally volunteered to map which streets most needed advice, using anonymous information and rough street boundaries gathered from households. She loved spreadsheets, but the first paper form was a failure. [51]After several residents skipped the questions about heating type, she redesigned the form with pictures of boilers, storage heaters and electric radiators.[/51] The answers became much more reliable almost immediately. Leila insisted that no names appeared on the public online map, because community trust mattered more than detail. She also checked that colours on the map could be understood by colour-blind readers. [52]The most rewarding moment came when a pensioner told the team she had stopped sleeping in her coat after receiving the right support.[/52] Leila says data can sound cold, but in this project it pointed volunteers towards people who were genuinely cold during winter. She now trains others to question neat-looking figures carefully before acting on them.
texts.D:
### Owen, The Installer Coordinator
Owen helped coordinate volunteers who fitted draught strips and radiator panels. [53]He joined because he remembered visiting his grandmother’s icy flat and feeling angry that she pretended it was comfortable.[/53] The memory made him patient with residents who said they did not want fuss. His main weekly job was organising appointments, tools and follow-up visits. [54]He often had to schedule installations around babies’ naps, night-shift workers’ sleep and carers’ brief visits.[/54] In practice, that meant a simple hallway job could take weeks to arrange. [55]Owen created a picture guide showing how to use new thermostat settings, because several residents were nervous about touching the controls afterwards.[/55] He checked back by phone a few days later, usually in the evening. [56]By spring, he was training street champions to handle basic booking calls, so the project would not slow down whenever he was unavailable.[/56] He found that shared responsibility reduced pressure for everyone.

questions.question: found that a home’s main source of cold air was not where the resident expected?
questions.explanation: Inez discovered the draught came through the oversized letterbox rather than the windows.

questions.question: used technical professional knowledge to explain a problem without criticising residents?
questions.explanation: Inez used civil engineering knowledge to explain heat loss without blaming residents.

questions.question: joined after seeing a local handwritten notice about the project?
questions.explanation: Mark went to the volunteer meeting because of a handwritten library notice.

questions.question: was surprised by a common misunderstanding about what caused high bills?
questions.explanation: Mark found many residents blamed sockets rather than heating and poor insulation.

questions.question: improved a form by replacing unclear written categories with pictures?
questions.explanation: Leila redesigned the form with pictures of different heating systems.

questions.question: recalls a resident saying support had changed how they slept at night?
questions.explanation: A pensioner said she had stopped sleeping in her coat after receiving support.

questions.question: was motivated by a memory of an older relative living in a cold home?
questions.explanation: Owen remembered his grandmother’s icy flat.

questions.question: had to arrange practical visits around household routines and caring responsibilities?
questions.explanation: Owen scheduled installations around babies’ naps, night shifts and carers’ visits.

questions.question: created visual guidance for people worried about changing controls?
questions.explanation: Owen made a picture guide for thermostat settings.

questions.question: trained local helpers to manage basic arrangements when he was not free?
questions.explanation: Owen trained street champions to handle basic booking calls.
